headline: From Playgrounds to Protection: A Philippine Community Saves Sea Turtles
meta_description: In Palawan, a local man who once played with turtle eggs as a child now leads efforts to save the vulnerable Olive Ridley sea turtles nesting on his beaches.
summary_short: A childhood spent treating sea turtle eggs like toys has transformed into a mission to protect them. In the Philippines, one community is fighting to save an endangered…
body:
As a child in Santo Niño village, Jerald Mahusay had no idea why sea turtle eggs mattered. "We were always playing with the eggs," he recalled. "We didn't know why they are important."

That casual indifference has become urgent purpose. Today, Mahusay heads the local patrol unit for the Large Marine Vertebrates Research Institute Philippines (LAMAVE), protecting Olive Ridley sea turtles in San Vicente, a municipality in Palawan that serves as a vital nesting ground for this vulnerable species.

The work is demanding and often heartbreaking. During peak nesting season, patrollers walk more than 8 kilometers each night across five shifts, searching for nesting turtles. When they find one attacked by stray dogs—the most immediate threat—they wait for the injured turtle to die so they can rescue her eggs. Those eggs go to one of two hatcheries, where researchers recreate the conditions of natural nests until the hatchlings emerge after about two months.

But the threats keep multiplying. Rising tides and stronger storms driven by climate change endanger nests left on beaches. Meanwhile, luxury developers have purchased over 500 hectares to build beachfront resorts, transforming the landscape where these turtles will eventually return.

What gives hope is the shift in community thinking—including Munding, a landowner whose eight dogs once threatened nests, now joining patrols to witness the effort firsthand. Change, it seems, comes from understanding what matters.
